name: Pediatrics: CH 21 The Child's Experience of Hospitalization

scenarios.question: During Piaget's sensorimotor stage, children primarily learn through:
options: Symbolic play and egocentric thinking
Sensory experiences and motor actions
Logical operations on concrete objects
Abstract reasoning and hypothesis testing
scenarios.rationale: The sensorimotor stage (birth-2 years) is characterized by learning through sensory experiences and motor actions. Infants develop object permanence and begin intentional actions. (Slide 21)

scenarios.question: Which statement best describes Extended or long?
options: This only applies to adult patients, not children
This is not a recognized term in pediatric nursing
This is an outdated concept no longer taught
term care facility Urgent care center Outpatient clinic or office Pediatric rese...
scenarios.rationale: Extended or long: term care facility Urgent care center Outpatient clinic or office Pediatric research center Outpatient surgery center Home === NOTES === Give examples (Slide 3)

scenarios.rationale: School: Aged Child Give an example of a situation in which the nurse allows the child to examine or play with a piece of equipment prior to using it (Slide 4)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Use the same approach as with adult patients
Exclude family members from care decisions
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
can lessen the stress of hospitalization for the child’s parents
scenarios.rationale: can lessen the stress of hospitalization for the child’s parents. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 2)

scenarios.question: What is the primary fear of hospitalized preschoolers?
options: Strangers
Missing school
Separation from parents and bodily injury
Death
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers: separation anxiety and fear of pain/injury are primary stressors. Encourage parental presence. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child's understanding of death depends on:
options: Previous experience with death
Parental explanation
Developmental age and cognitive level
Religion only
scenarios.rationale: Death concept: preschool — reversible; school-age — permanent but distant; adolescent — abstract, universal. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which is an effective way to prepare a child for hospitalization?
options: Therapeutic play with medical equipment
Using medical jargon
Showing graphic photos
Not telling them anything
scenarios.rationale: Medical play (dolls, equipment) helps children express fears, gain mastery. Age-appropriate preparation reduces anxiety. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the best way to explain a procedure to a 4-year-old?
options: Show graphic videos
Use simple terms, dolls, and short explanations
Give detailed medical explanation
Have parents explain
scenarios.rationale: Preschoolers: short, concrete explanations; dolls or drawings; demonstrate on a teddy bear. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A hospitalized child regresses to bedwetting. The nurse should:
options: Punish the child
Call child protective services
Reassure parents this is normal regression
Ignore it
scenarios.rationale: Regression (bedwetting, thumb sucking) is a normal coping mechanism during stress. Reassure parents it's temporary. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which intervention best supports family-centered care?
options: Limiting family visiting hours
Having family leave during procedures
Treating only the child
Including family in care decisions and teaching
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered care: families are partners in care; include in decisions, teaching, and presence. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A 7-year-old undergoing surgery is worried about waking up. What should the nurse say?
options: 'The doctor will make sure you wake up after surgery'
'Don't worry about it'
'You might not wake up'
'Ask your parents'
scenarios.rationale: Concrete, honest reassurance. Address fears directly and simply. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the best approach when a child is uncooperative during a procedure?
options: Give up and try later
Hold the child down
Threaten punishment
Explain what will happen and allow choices when possible
scenarios.rationale: Allow choices (which arm for shot), use distraction, explain simply. Avoid restraint unless necessary. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which age group benefits most from a tour of the hospital before admission?
options: All ages equally
Adolescents
Preschoolers and school-age children
Infants
scenarios.rationale: Preparation tours with dolls/equipment help 3-12 year olds. Adolescents may prefer verbal info. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child's favorite toy from home should be:
options: Encouraged and kept with the child
Placed in a locker
Disinfected hourly
Taken away to prevent loss
scenarios.rationale: Transitional objects (blanket, teddy) provide comfort and security. Keep with child. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Match each developmental theorist with their key concept.
scenarios.rationale: Erikson: psychosocial crises. Piaget: cognitive development. Kohlberg: moral reasoning. (Slide 15-20)

scenarios.question: Complete the statement about pediatric care.
scenarios.cloze_text: Pediatric nursing requires [[BLANK1]] care and consideration of [[BLANK2]] in all interactions.
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered, age-appropriate care is fundamental in pediatrics. (Slide 10)

scenarios.question: Click on the normal developmental findings.
scenarios.highlight_text: The child demonstrates age-appropriate milestones. Growth follows expected percentiles. The child engages with caregivers appropriately.
scenarios.rationale: All are normal pediatric findings. (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: A child presents with concerning developmental findings.

options: Perform developmental screening
Obtain detailed history
Ignore parental concerns
Delay assessment
Possible developmental concern requiring evaluation
Normal variation, no action needed
Immediate emergency
Follow-up assessments
Parent education
No follow-up needed

scenarios.rationale: Early identification and family support are key. (Slide 30)
